coredata.dc:title: Silicon-doped multilayer graphene as anode material for secondary batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Shouhua
Li, Boqin
Yang, Zhen
Song, Yitian
Wang, Gang
Yu, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Silicon oxycarbide (SiOC) is considered a promising anode material for lithium-ion batteries. However, the complexity of conventional SiOC synthesis methods and the significant volume expansion of Si limit its commercial application. In this study, graphene flakes were successfully doped with Si atoms through an in-situ reaction between SiCl4 (photovoltaic waste) and hydroxyl-rich carbon nanodots as the carbon precursor, generating SiOC bonds. The obtained Si-doped multilayer graphene material exhibited enhanced specific capacitance compared with the pristine graphene flake. Moreover, the Si volume expansion challenge was effectively addressed, owing to the presence of SiOC bonds and the stable structure of the graphene sheets. Notably, the anode material maintained a specific discharge capacity of 653 mAh·g−1 even after 500 charge–discharge cycles at 100 mA·g−1. This excellent specific capacity is mainly attributed to the provision of more Li+ active sites by the SiOC network and the high pseudocapacitance of the material.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Si doping
Graphene sheets
In situ synthesis
Anode materials
Li-ion batteries

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the preparation of MLG and Si-MLG materials; (b) TEM images of CND; (c), (d) SEM and (e), (f) TEM images of MLG; (g), (h) SEM and (i), (j) TEM images of Si-MLG; (k) TEM image and corresponding EDS mappings of Si-MLG; (l) XRD, (m) Raman of MLG and Si-MLG.
XPS (a) full scan of MLG and Si-MLG; C 1 s of (b) MLG and (c) Si-MLG; (d) Si 2p of Si-MLG; O 1 s of (e) MLG and (f) Si-MLG; N 1 s of (g) MLG and (h) Si-MLG; (i) FT-IR of MLG and Si-MLG.

(a) CV profiles of Si-MLG obtained at scan rates of 0.2–1.0 mV s−1; (b) measurement of the b values for the anodic and cathodic peaks of the Si-MLG electrode; (c) capacitance contribution at various scan rates for Si-MLG; (d) separation of capacitive and diffusion-controlled currents at 1.0 mV s−1 for Si-MLG; (e) the charge–discharge profiles of Si-MLG; (f) Nyquist plots of MLG and Si-MLG after initial charging; (g) cycling performances of MLG and Si-MLG at 100 mA·g−1; (h) rate capabilities of MLG and Si-MLG.
SEM images of the Si-MLG electrode (a), (b) before and (c), (d) after 500 cycles at a current density of 100 mA·g−1.
Various heteroatom-doped carbon materials for lithium-ion batteries.

head.ce:title: Silicon-doped multilayer graphene as anode material for secondary batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Green Processing of Chemical Engineering of Xinjiang Bingtuan, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shihezi University, Shihezi 832003, China

School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory for Green Processing of Chemical Engineering of Xinjiang Bingtuan, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shihezi University, Shihezi 832003, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Green Processing of Chemical Engineering of Xinjiang Bingtuan
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silicon oxycarbide (SiOC) is considered a promising anode material for lithium-ion batteries. However, the complexity of conventional SiOC synthesis methods and the significant volume expansion of Si limit its commercial application. In this study, graphene flakes were successfully doped with Si atoms through an in-situ reaction between SiCl4 (photovoltaic waste) and hydroxyl-rich carbon nanodots as the carbon precursor, generating SiOC bonds. The obtained Si-doped multilayer graphene material exhibited enhanced specific capacitance compared with the pristine graphene flake. Moreover, the Si volume expansion challenge was effectively addressed, owing to the presence of SiOC bonds and the stable structure of the graphene sheets. Notably, the anode material maintained a specific discharge capacity of 653 mAh·g−1 even after 500 charge–discharge cycles at 100 mA·g−1. This excellent specific capacity is mainly attributed to the provision of more Li+ active sites by the SiOC network and the high pseudocapacitance of the material.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are the most popular portable energy-storage devices because of their low mass density and high energy density [1,2]. However, the extremely low theoretical specific capacity (372 mAh·g−1) of the current commercial graphite anode has made it difficult to meet market demand [3]. To meet the requirements of electric vehicles, materials with high specific capacities, high power densities, and good Coulombic efficiencies have been intensively researched worldwide. Among them, Si is considered to be a promising anode material for lithium-ion batteries because it undergoes a reversible alloying reaction during the charging and discharging process of lithium-ion batteries as follows:
S i + x
L i
+
+ x
e
-
↔
L i
x
S i
, and when a fully lithiated alloy of Li22Si5 is formed, its theoretical specific capacity is as high as 4200 mAh·g−1 [4,5], and Si is abundant on Earth [6]. However, its direct application as an anode remains challenging because of its poor conductivity and notable volume expansion (>300 %) during cycling. These drawbacks lead to severe anode polarization, increased interfacial resistance, continuum breakage, and poor cycling performance due to solid-electrolyte interface (SEI) formation [7,8]. There are many methods to solve the volume expansion problem of Si-based materials. The most common strategy involves blending Si with C, resulting in materials with high electrical conductivity and excellent cycling stability [9]. Various Si/C composite structures, such as graphene/Si multilayer structures [10], carbon nanotube/Si (CNT/Si) membranes [11], Si/C core–shell structures [12], Si/C yolk-shell hollow structures [13], and pomegranate structures [14], have been reported to better accommodate Si volume expansion. However, their widespread application is hindered by challenges, such as a high resistance at the Si/C interface as well as a drastic decrease in the volume-specific capacitance and Coulombic efficiency, underscoring the need for novel Si/C composites [15].

Recently, graphene sheets have been widely recognized as an ideal carbon substrate for Si nanoparticles and have shown great potential for application as an anode material in LIBs [16,17]. Due to its high electronic conductivity, graphene sheets can act as an efficient electron transport channel, significantly improving the charge transfer efficiency within batteries. In addition, graphene exhibits good mechanical flexibility, which is crucial in Li-ion batteries. It effectively mitigates the volume changes of Si nanoparticles during charge–discharge cycles, thereby maintaining the stability of the anode material and prolonging the battery life [18]. In Si/C anode materials for LIBs, Si atoms co-exist with carbon in various forms, mainly SiOx/C composites, SiC crystals, and Si/C composites. Among them, SiOx/C composites, which consist of glassy silicon oxides with glassy carbon (SiOxCy) and free carbon phases, have been considered promising anode candidates for LIBs [19]. Lei et al. [20] prepared rhodochrosite-like, vertical, graphene-coated, hollow porous SiOC (Hp-SiOC@VG) spherical particles by hydrothermal treatment combined with methane pyrolysis. The particles exhibited high reversible specific capacitance, excellent cycling stability, and high multiplicity capability in LIBs. Liu et al. [21] employed polycarbosilane as a precursor and obtained a silica-oxycarbon ceramic material by a two-step process involving thermal oxidation in air and heat treatment. The resulting anode material exhibited high cycling stability and ICE of up to 77 % at high current densities. Although SiOx/C composites are competitive anode materials, the formation of SiOC bonds typically involves complex processes [22,23], hindering their large-scale commercialization.

Therefore, we propose a simple strategy involving the in situ doping of Si atoms into multilayer graphene (MLG), forming SiOC bonds. Accordingly, carbon nanodots (CND) were synthesized by the heat treatment of citric acid and urea using the conventional microwave-assisted method. Subsequently, the CND were carbonized to produce MLG by simple heat treatment. After the initial carbonization at 300 °C, the resulting CND were rich in hydroxyl groups [24]. Next, Si-doped multilayer graphene (Si-MLG) enriched with SiOC bonds was synthesized by high-temperature carbonization. In this process, the hydroxyl groups and SiCl4 (photovoltaic waste) were subjected to a nucleophilic addition reaction, forming SiOC bonds. The pristine MLG material was employed as the anode material in the LIBs, and it maintained a specific discharge capacity of 528 mAh·g−1 after 500 cycles at 100 mA·g−1. Similarly, Si-MLG with the SiOC network was adopted, and it maintained a specific discharge capacity of 653 mAh·g−1 after 500 cycles at 100 mA·g−1, significantly higher than that of the pristine MLG. The SiOC network formed within the MLG after Si doping provided additional Li+ active sites, which enhanced the specific capacitance and significantly reduced the Si bulk expansion. The prepared Si-MLG material demonstrated excellent anode performance in LIBs, highlighting its potential for adoption in industrial applications.

Chemicals:Chemicals: Urea (CH4N2O, 99.0 %), citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.5 %), silicon tetrachloride (SiCl4, 99.5 %), and N-methylpyrrolidone (C5H9NO, 99.5 %) were purchased from McLean Corporation. All reagents are of analytical grade and all chemicals are used without further purification.

Preparation of CND: For this, a typical scheme from the literature was employed [24]. Five-gram portions of citric acid and urea were dissolved in 7.5 mL of deionized water, after which the reaction mixture was heated in a household microwave oven for 10 min. The microwaved samples were ground in an alumina crucible, heated to 300℃ at 6 ℃/min under an argon atmosphere, and held at this temperature for 2 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the CND were ground.

Preparation of MLG: The CND (1.0 g) were placed in the quartz tube of a tube furnace and heated to 800 °C at 5 °C/min under an argon atmosphere. The temperature was maintained at 800 °C for 3 h. The MLG material was obtained by grinding the resulting sample after natural cooling to room temperature.

Preparation of Si-MLG: The CND (1.0 g) were dissolved in N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP) and stirred until complete dissolution. Thereafter, 0.3 mL of SiCl4 was slowly added dropwise, following which the obtained colloid was transferred to a vacuum oven and dried at a constant temperature of 35℃ for 12 h and subsequently at 80℃ for 12 h. The dried material was ground to obtain the Si-CND powder. Next, the powder material was placed in the quartz tube of the tube furnace and heated to 800℃ at 5 ℃/min under an argon atmosphere at a constant temperature for 3 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the Si-MLG material was milled.

The surface morphology of the materials was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU 8020). The micromorphology and internal structure of the materials were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEl Tecnai G2 F30). The graphitization degree and surface defects of the samples were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 ADVANCE). The elemental composition and bonding state of the sample surfaces were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi). The graphitization degree and crystal structure of the samples were evaluated by Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw inVia). The functional-group composition of the materials was analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet iS10).

The active material, binder (polyvinylidene difluoride), and conductive agent (conductive carbon black, Super P) were weighed in an 8:1:1 mass ratio. The materials were placed in 5 mL sample tubes, after which the appropriate amount of NMP was introduced using a pipette gun. Magnets were added to the prepared slurry, followed by magnetic stirring at 400 rpm for 6 h. The copper foil (thickness = 9 μm) was cut into rectangular shapes and fixed on an adapted glass plate with tape. The uniformly dispersed slurry was applied to the smooth surface of the copper foil. The coated copper foil was placed in a vacuum oven and dried at 80 °C for 12 h. The uncoated portion of the copper foil was also cut into at least five pieces and weighed, and the mean value was used to determine the weight of each coated small round pole piece by calculating the difference.

The battery assembly process was performed in a glove box filled with high purity argon. The necessary experimental materials, such as positive and negative battery cases, lithium metal foil (15.6 * 0.45 mm), separator, gaskets (15.8 * 1.0 mm), spring (15.4 * 1.1 mm) and electrolyte, were placed in the glove box prior to the experiment, ensuring that the water and oxygen content of the box met the standard (O2 ≤ 0.1 ppm, H2O ≤ 0.1 ppm). The batteries were assembled in the following order: positive case, lithium metal foil (d = 15.6 mm), electrolyte (LiPF6), separator (Celgard 2400, d = 16.0 mm), electrolyte, negative tabs (d = 10.0 mm), gaskets, spring, and negative case. Using insulated tweezers, the charged battery was placed in a sample-sealing bag and sealed with a sealing machine. Next, the battery’s surface was wiped clean, after which it was left at room temperature for 24 h to allow the electrolyte to infiltrate the pole piece completely.

Charge-discharge tests can show the cycling characteristics, specific capacity, and rate-performance of an electrode material. In this study, the LANHE battery test system was employed to achieve the following conditions: voltage range, 0.005–3.0 V; long-cycle constant charge–discharge current density, 100 mA·g−1; rate-performance test current densities, 100, 200, 300, 500, 1000, and 100 mA g−1. An electrochemical workstation was employed for cyclic voltammetry (CV). The test voltage range was 0.005–3.0 V, and the sweep rate was 0.1 mV s−1. The electrochemical workstation was used to test the AC impedance over a frequency range of 0.01–106 Hz, with a voltage amplitude of 0.005 V, starting from the measured open circuit voltage of the cell.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, hydroxyl-rich CND materials were synthesized using a conventional method [24], and sheet-like MLG materials were further carbonized and synthesized. And through the nucleophilic addition reaction between hydroxyl-rich CND and SiCl4, stable SiOC bonds are formed. High-temperature carbonization not only enables successful doping of Si atoms but also preserves the lamellar structure of multilayer graphene, leading to the successful preparation of Si-MLG materials rich in SiOC bonds. The TEM image of CNDs in Fig. 1b shows particles in the range of 10 to 50 nm. After the high-temperature carbonization of the CND, as shown in the SEM results in Fig. 1c, d, g, and h, both the MLG and Si-MLG materials exhibited irregular sheet-like structures with sizes ranging from hundreds of nanometers to a few micrometers. A comparison shows that the Si-MLG material has more wrinkles, attributable to the difference in the thermal expansion coefficients of its components. The Si doping may have caused a change in the thermal expansion coefficients of the carbon materials, leading to inhomogeneous expansion during the high-temperature treatment and ultimately resulting in the wrinkling of the laminations.

TEM images also confirmed that the MLG and Si-MLG materials had irregular sheet-like structures with sizes of a few hundred nanometers (Fig. 1e, i). As clearly shown in Fig. 1f, j, the surface of the material after high-temperature carbonization exhibits typical graphene stripes, further validating the successful synthesis of multilayer graphene. Fig. 1k shows that the Si-MLG materials feature uniformly dispersed Si elements, in addition to C, O, and N. This observation is related to the reaction of SiCl4 with the hydroxyl groups enriched in the CND to form stable SiOC chemical bonds, effectively preventing Si agglomeration during the reaction process and promoting its dispersion. This highly dispersed Si element plays an active role in retarding the expansion during LIB storage, helping to improve battery performance and stability.

The crystal structure and crystallographic information of the samples were observed by XRD analysis, and the results are shown in Fig. 1l. Two distinct peaks appear at 25.71° and 43.35° for MLG and Si-MLG, and the distances of the two peaks at the positions calculated by Bragg's equation are 0.355 nm and 0.225 nm, corresponding to the (002) and (101) crystallographic planes of graphene, respectively [25,26]. In addition, the layer spacing of the (002) crystallographic plane in Si-MLG is 0.355 nm, which is larger than the 0.335 nm of conventional graphene, mainly due to the doping of N and Si atoms [27]. In the Raman spectrum of Fig. 1m, two distinct peaks can be observed at ∼ 1351 cm−1 (D peak) and ∼ 1527 cm−1 (G peak) [28,29]. The relative intensity (ID/IG) of MLG and Si-MLG samples is close to 1.0, indicating that the graphene layer has a high defect density or a highly disordered structure. A 2D peak characteristic of graphene is observed at 2700 cm−1 for all samples.

The XPS results are shown in Fig. 2 . In the full Si-MLG spectrum (Fig. 2a), Si 2p and Si 2 s peaks can be observed at 103 eV and 153 eV, respectively, and the doping amount of Si is 4.53 wt%. In Fig. 2b and c, C 1 s peak signals of sample c can be observed at 284.65, 285.68, 286.62, and 289.85 eV, corresponding to the C = C/C–C, C-O/C-N, C = O, and O-C = O bonds, respectively [30]. Analysis of the Si 2p peak in the Si-MLG spectrum (Fig. 2d), it is clear that it can be split into two peaks at 102.87 and 103.39 eV, corresponding to the Si and C network structures of SiO3C and SiO4 [31,32], respectively. These indicate that the Si atoms were successfully doped into the network structure of the carbon material and that they exist in the MLG material in the form of SiOxC4-x bonds. The SiOC network not only provides new storage sites for Li+ to enhance the specific capacitance performance of the carbon materials, but also, its unique structure effectively inhibits the volume expansion of Si atoms, significantly improving the cycle stability and durability of the materials.

As shown in Fig. 2e, the O 1 s peak of MLG can be fitted to two split peaks at 530.83 and 532.22 eV, corresponding to the C = O and C-O bonds, respectively [33]. As shown in Fig. 2f, the O 1 s peak of Si-MLG, in addition to fitting the two peaks mentioned above, produces a new peak at 533.00 eV, corresponding to the O-Si bond [34]. This further confirms the formation of Si-O bonds. The N 1 s peaks shown in Fig. 2g, h can be fitted to two split peaks at 398.39 and 400.65 eV, corresponding to the two forms of nitrogen present in typical N-doped carbon materials: pyridinic nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen, respectively [35].

In addition, the chemical bonds and functional groups of the samples were analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 2i. Both MLG and Si-MLG display characteristic peaks at 3435, 1628, and 1111 cm−1, corresponding to the –OH, C = C/C = O, and C–C bonds, respectively [36]. More importantly, the signal at 1064 cm−1 confirms the presence of C-O-Si bonds [37], indicating that Si atoms were successfully doped into the backbone of the porous carbon material. The signal at 816 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration peak of the C-Si bond [38].

The electrochemical properties of both materials were investigated using CV curves. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the CV curves of Si-MLG show similar intercalation/deintercalation peaks at different scanning rates. The positions of the peaks gradually shift forward as the scanning rate increases, a trend that not only reveals the polarization phenomenon in its electrochemical behavior but also further confirms its fast electrochemical kinetics. Notably, the pronounced reduction peaks between 1.0 and 0.5 V in the first cycle are due to the formation of the SEI layer, while the peaks below 0.5 V are mainly due to Li+ storage. The CV curves were recorded at different sweep rates of 0.2–1.0 mV·s−1, and the value of b was calculated using the formula i = aνb to investigate the Li+ storage mechanism and kinetics of the Si-MLG flake. A b value of 0.5 indicates Li+ diffusion, while a value of 1.0 indicates an artifactual capacitance-driven process [39]. The calculated results are shown in Fig. 3b, where the b values of the anode and cathode peaks are 0.77 and 0.76, respectively. This indicates that the Si-MLG anode material undergoes a hybrid kinetic process involving ion-diffusion and pseudocapacitance control in the LIB. In terms of capacity, ion-diffusion ensures that the Si-MLG anode material can store a considerable amount of lithium ions to achieve a high energy density and capacity.

In addition, the mixing dynamics favor the cycling stability of the battery anode material and extend the battery life. The pseudocapacitance contribution of Si-MLG is calculated by the equation i(V) = k1ν + k2ν1/2, where i is the current measured at a fixed potential (V), k1 and k2 are constants, and ν is the scan rate [40,41]. The charge storage contributions of Si-MLG at different scan rates are shown in Fig. 3c, d. The capacitive contribution tends to increase with the scan rate, reaching 89.7 % at 1.0 mV s−1. The pseudocapacitance is mainly attributed to the unique SiOC bonding, which allows the uniform doping of Si atoms in the 3D graphene sheet structure. This not only ensures that this anode material can have good cycling stability but also provides an abundance of active sites and Si doping defects, leading to additional Li+ storage. Furthermore, the high capacitance contribution enables the rapid diffusion of Li+ in the Si-MLG anode material without destroying the electrode structure.

As shown in Fig. 3e, the shape of the charge–discharge curve agrees well with the CV curve in the voltage range of 0.01–3.0 V. The discharge capacity of the Si-MLG anode material was approximately 1333.7 mAh·g−1 during the first discharge. However, during the second cycle, a significant and irreversible decrease in the specific charge capacity to 551.0 mAh·g−1 was observed, with an initial coulomb efficiency of only 41.3 %. The main reason for this may be the presence of a large number of oxygen- and hydrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of graphene sheets, which irreversibly react with lithium ions during the first cycle [42]. In addition, Li+ can become trapped in defect sites and pores formed in the Si-doped carbon structure during initial cycling [43]. Moreover, the formation of SEI membranes consumes additional lithium, further reducing first-cycle coulombic efficiency [44]. Fig. 3f shows a comparison of the impedance profiles before and after the introduction of Si atoms. The results show that the semicircular diameter of Si-MLG is larger than that of MLG in the first half of the high-frequency region. This suggests that the charge transfer impedance between the electrolyte and the electrode is increased in the Si-MLG material compared with that for pure MLG, which may affect the electrochemical performance. However, the effect of the higher impedance of Si itself is reduced by the fact that Si doped into the graphene layer can provide good conductive channels through the graphene layer. More importantly, the theoretical specific capacity of Si (approximately 4200 mAh·g−1) is significantly higher than that of carbon materials, providing more sites for Li+ intercalation and deintercalation. Thus, despite the increase in resistance, the overall capacitance of the material still increases as Si is doped.

In the low-current cycling test (Fig. 3g), the Si-MLG anode material maintains a specific capacity of up to 653 mAh·g−1 after 500 cycles, whereas the pristine MLG exhibits a specific capacity of only 528 mAh·g−1. The overall high Li storage capacity of the Si-MLG anode material is mainly due to the doping of a moderate amount of Si atoms with a high theoretical specific capacitance. When Si is bonded to O to form SiOxC4-x, it reacts with Li+ as follows [45,46]:
(1)

(2)

↔ 0.25
L i
4

O 4
+
1 - 0.25 x
S i
(3)
S i
O x
+ 0.4 x
L i
+
+ 0.4 x

↔ 0.2 x

1 - 0.4 x
S i
(4)
S i + x
L i

+ x

L i
x
S i
When Si is present in the form of SiOxC4-x, it introduces new Li+ intercalation and deintercalation sites, thereby increasing the specific capacitance of the carbon material. There are relevant reports indicating that Li+ has a higher tendency to adsorb onto SiO4 and SiO3C [19], thereby storing lithium ions more efficiently and increasing the specific capacity of anode materials. The Si 2p spectra in Fig. 2d indicate that our in situ synthesized Si-doped carbon anode materials contain only SiO4 and SiO3C structures, further suggesting that Si-MLG has structural advantages that can effectively enhance the specific capacity of carbon materials. The specific capacitance of the Si-MLG is well maintained after 500 charge–discharge cycles, mainly due to the effective limitation of Si volume expansion by the SiOC bonds and the stable MLG structure. These two properties work synergistically to solve the Si volume expansion problem in LIBs. The SiOC bonds effectively suppress the Si volume change during charging and discharging by enhancing the interfacial interaction between Si atoms and MLG. Meanwhile, the MLG, with its unique layered structure and excellent mechanical properties, provides solid support for the entire composite. This ensures that the Si-MLG maintains excellent electrochemical performance even after many cycles.

In the rate-capability performance plots shown in Fig. 3h, both MLG and Si-MLG samples exhibit a certain decreasing trend with increasing current, accompanied by fluctuations during some cycles. However, as the number of cycles continues to increase, the two materials gradually show stable performance. This phenomenon is most likely due to electrolyte decomposition and SEI film formation, which is a common phenomenon that occurs during battery cycling [47]. The current density recovered from a high current density of 1C to a low current density of 0.1C, and the specific capacitance exhibited good recovery as well, highlighting the good reversibility and recovery ability of both materials.

The structure of the Si-MLG electrode before and after cycling was characterized by scanning electron microscopy to explore the structural stability of this anode material during long-term cycling, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . A comparison of Fig. 4a, b and Fig. 4c, d shows that the surface of the Si-MLG electrode is initially rough, but after 500 cycles, it becomes smooth and dense. This transformation is attributed to the dense SEI film that forms on the electrode surface during the initial charge–discharge process. The absence of cracks on the surface of the Si-MLG electrode is observed in Fig. 4c, d, indicating that Li+ did not significantly damage the electrode structure during lithium storage. This is mainly due to the stable SiOC bonds and the robust structure of multilayer graphene, which limit the volume expansion of Si atoms, thereby protecting the electrode and giving it excellent cycling stability. And in Fig. 4b, it can be observed that there are many nanosheets stacked on the surface of the electrode, forming a dense lamellar layer. This lamellar structure can effectively facilitate the intercalation and de-intercalation of Li+, thereby enhancing the cycling stability.

To fully evaluate the electrochemical performance of Si-MLG anode materials, Table 1 summarizes recent research on the electrochemical performance of Si-doped carbon materials used as anodes in Li-ion batteries. Comparisons of cycle life and reversible specific capacity indicate that Si-MLG anode materials have certain advantages over Si-doped carbon anodes synthesized by conventional methods.

In this work, Si-MLG materials enriched with SiOC bonds were successfully synthesized using a facile strategy involving in situ doping and thermal treatment. The formation of SiOC bonds was confirmed by XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy. The SiOC bond was mainly formed by the in situ doping reaction between SiCl4 (photovoltaic waste) and hydroxyl-rich CND, while the MLG structure was formed by heat treatment of the CND. The Si-MLG enriched with SiOC bonds exhibited excellent anode performance in LIBs, maintaining a specific discharge capacity of 653 mAh·g−1 after 500 charge–discharge cycles at 100 mA·g−1. Compared with the pristine MLG materials, the Si-MLG material featured more Li+ active sites, provided by the SiOC bonds, which resulted in increased specific capacitance. The introduction of the SiOC bonds effectively limited the Si volume expansion during the charge–discharge cycles, thereby significantly improving the cycling stability of the anode material. This resolved the issue of rapid capacity degradation commonly associated with Si-based anodes due to volume expansion. This paper presents a simple method for preparing Si-doped anode materials with high capacitance and low volume expansion.

Shouhua Yang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Boqin Li: Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Zhen Yang: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Yitian Song: Resources, Methodology. Gang Wang: Visualization, Investigation. Feng Yu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Xinjiang Science and Technology Program (2023TSYCCX0118) and Bingtuan Science and Technology Program (No. 2023CB008-21).
